exemplar.title: My aim of this mathematical exploration is to utilize and apply my mathematical skills in solving differential equations and calculus, to predict the outcomes of a of hornetâs attack on a honeybee colony. This will be done by applying Lanchesterâs war model.

detailedExemplarData.title: My aim of this mathematical exploration is to utilize and apply my mathematical skills in solving differential equations and calculus, to predict the outcomes of a of hornetâs attack on a honeybee colony. This will be done by applying Lanchesterâs war model.

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear logical progression from introduction through modelling, solution and conclusion
Wellâstructured with headings, subheadings, table of contents and numbered equations
Labels on figures and consistent sectioning aid navigability
weaknesses: Overly lengthy background anecdotes reduce conciseness
Conclusion restates material verbatim and could be more focused
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands achieved Band 3 for coherence and organization, demonstrating solid logical development and structure. Conciseness lagged (Band 2) due to unnecessary anecdotes and verbose conclusion. On balance, a level 3 reflects the largely coherent, well-organized presentation with room to tighten content.

strengths: Almost universally correct use of mathematical notation and language; variables are defined
Arguments are presented in clear, sequential steps with numbered equations
Logical structure and clarity are strong, guiding the reader through derivations
weaknesses: Limited variety of representations (only two graphs, no tables or sensitivity visuals)
Occasional informal notations (e.g., ââ equation 1â) and inconsistent prime usage
Some omitted intermediate steps reduce transparency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates consistent and accurate mathematical language and a clear logical structure (Band 3â4). Although multiple representations are only moderately varied (Band 2), the overall clarity and near-error-free symbolism justify a top-band score of 4 in Mathematical Communication.

strengths: Choice of applying Lanchesterâs law to beeâhornet conflict shows significant independent thinking
Personal narrative and justification of parameter choices give a clear individual stamp
Creative ecological context and adaptation of military model demonstrate initiative
weaknesses: No original data collection or experimentation to deepen personal approach
Model pathway largely follows textbook procedures
Exploration stops at a single parameter set without extended creative analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands sit firmly at Band 2: the work shows clear evidence of independent thinking, personal engagement and creativity, but lacks the innovative depth or experimental initiative required for Band 3. A score of 2 accurately reflects this level.

strengths: Acknowledges model limitations (negative populations) and domain restrictions
Identifies that certain assumptions (e.g. constant coefficients) may invalidate results
weaknesses: Reflection is superficial with no deep interrogation of assumptions
Critical analysis of results is minimal and underdeveloped
No substantive evaluation of outcomes or proposed refinements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each reflection strand was assessed at Band 1: limited depth, basic critical analysis and cursory outcome evaluation. The student recognises some limitations but fails to develop meaningful or comprehensive reflection, justifying a score of 1.

strengths: Correct derivation and solution of coupled ODEs with characteristic equations
Flawless derivation of invariants and verification that solutions satisfy the differential equation
Mathematics is fully at HL level and applied effectively to the ecological model
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in units and sign conventions
Calculation of constants Câ and Câ not fully confirmed by back-substitution
No exploration of non-linear extensions or numerical methods
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Work consistently uses mathematics commensurate with HL level and demonstrates thorough understanding (Band 4â5). While not pushing into expert or numerical methods (Band 6), the depth of ODE manipulation, invariant derivation and contextual interpretation warrant a high score of 5.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations are broken down into sequential steps and supported by numbered equations, allowing the reader to follow the derivation from the coupled first-order system to the second-order ODE and its solution. Occasional omissions (units for time, abrupt jump from model to conclusion) limit complete clarity but logical structure remains good.

Derivations are generally correct and the student verifies the solution satisfies the differential equation. Minor inconsistencies (units for population change per minute, sign convention in graph interpretation) show understanding is good but not flawless; hence accuracy sits at the âmostly correctâ level rather than completely correct.

The student employs algebraic manipulation, differential-equation notation and two graphs to convey results, giving some variety. However, graphical representation is minimal (only end-point labels, no combined plot) and no tables or parameterâsensitivity visuals are provided, so the use of multiple representations is only moderate.

The student follows a clear progression from introduction through modelling, solution, evaluation and conclusion. Although occasional digressions and some misplaced references break the flow, the mathematical argument generally develops in a logical order and the reader can follow how each step leads to the next.

Selecting an ecological context and adjusting force coefficients exhibits some creativity and initiative. Nevertheless, the exploration stops after a single parameter set and does not extend to sensitivity analysis or alternative models, so creativity, while evident, remains at a good rather than exceptional level.

Critical analysis of results is brief, stating merely that a hive with more bees will win and identifying a few limitations. The student does not interrogate assumptions (constant coefficients, instantaneous engagement) nor assess sensitivity, keeping analysis at a basic level.

Outcome evaluation consists of acknowledging that prediction is difficult and that bee defences like heat-balling could alter results, but no quantitative reconsideration or extension is made. Consequently, evaluation remains limited.

Reflection is present but shallow: the student comments that the model is âinvalidâ for scouts and notes populations cannot be negative, yet offers no deeper discussion of why the mathematical structure fails or how it might be refined. Thus only limited reflection is demonstrated.

Mathematical symbols, derivatives, differentials and constants are nearly always written correctly and variables are defined. A few informal notations (e.g. ââ equation 1â) and inconsistent prime usage appear, but overall the language and symbolism are appropriate and mostly consistent.

Personal narrative about childhood curiosity and explicit justification of parameter choices provide a clear personal stamp. Yet the modelling pathway is fairly conventional, and no unique data collection or experimentation is attempted, so the approach is personal but not highly innovative.

The narrative contains sizeable background anecdotes and repeated explanations of standard solution methods, so the exploration is longer than necessary. Most material is relevant to modelling the beeâhornet interaction, yet some quotations and website extracts add little mathematical value, reducing conciseness.

The mathematicsâcoupled first-order differential equations, reduction to a second-order ODE, solution via characteristic equation and graphical interpretationâis well beyond basic algebra and squarely within HL syllabus expectations. It is applied effectively to address the stated aim, though limited parameter testing curbs comprehensiveness.

The choice of applying Lanchesterâs law to insect conflict shows the studentâs own interest and some adaptation of a military model, indicating notable independent thinking. The mathematics itself, however, follows textbook procedures closely, so evidence is significant but not outstanding.

Headings, sub-headings and a table of contents give the work a clear structure and each section is signposted. Figures are labelled and equations are numbered, aiding navigation. Minor lapses (e.g. duplicated references within text boxes) prevent it from reaching an exceptional standard but overall organisation is solid and purposeful.

Solving constant-coefficient linear ODEs, handling exponentials and interpreting them in context is fully commensurate with HL level. The work neither ventures into genuinely advanced techniques (e.g. non-linear systems, numerical methods) nor demonstrates mastery over a broad range, so a solid but not top band is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The main header is clear and professional, providing immediate context for the exploration, though adding a subtitle could further specify the focus on beeâhornet interaction.
The title is descriptive but could be more precise by mentioning the ecological context (e.g. modelling beeâhornet dynamics) to guide the reader.
The table of contents is introduced with a clear header, effectively signalling the structure of the document.
Entries in the table of contents lack page numbers, making navigation harder. Add page references for each section.
The introductory narrative is engaging but overly lengthy, with background anecdotes that distract from the mathematical focus.
The aim is stated succinctly and aligns clearly with the mathematical exploration that follows.
The descriptive text on Lanchesterâs laws omits formal mathematical definitions. Include explicit formulas for both linear and quadratic forms.
The choice to use the quadratic law is justified only qualitatively. Provide quantitative or empirical evidence (e.g. force coefficients) to support this decision.
Time variable t is introduced without specifying units. State consistently that t is measured in minutes.
Equation numbering is placed within the math display. For clarity, enclose equations in $$...$$ and label numbers outside the math environment.
The step eliminating dt in the ratio of derivatives is presented too tersely. Include a brief rationale for converting $$\frac{dB/dt}{dH/dt}$$ into $$\frac{dB}{dH}$$.
After integration, interpret constant C physically as the invariant difference $$\frac b2B^2-\frac h2H^2$$ in this ecological model.
The derivation of the invariant $$B^2\!:\!H^2$$ constant is flawless, showing a solid grasp of separable equations.
The context for the scouting attack is described verbally but the choice of initial hive size lacks quantitative justification; cite hive data or literature.
Parameters b=2 and h=8 are assumed without testing their impact. Perform a sensitivity analysis to evaluate model stability.
The jump to $$B''(t)=16B(t)$$ omits intermediate algebraic steps. Include the detailed substitution from $$H'(t)$$ to $$B''(t)$$.
The section header clearly marks the solution of the ODE, enhancing navigability and structure.
Before presenting the general solution, discuss the discriminantâs sign and why it leads to two real roots in this case.
The discriminant formula $$\Delta=b^2-4ac$$ is correctly stated, showing understanding of root classification.
The repeated-root solution is incorrect. For $$\Delta=0$$ the general solution should be $$y=(C_1+C_2x)e^{rx}$$, including the x multiplier.
Correctly identifies a=1, b=0, c=-16 in the standard ODE form, enabling an accurate characteristic equation.
Display the algebraic solution of constants C_1 and C_2 step by step instead of relying solely on a calculator for transparency.
Values of C_1 and C_2 should be confirmed by back-substitution into the original equation to ensure consistency.
The graph shows increasing bee numbers, contradicting expectations. Discuss restricting the domain or refining the model to address this.
Declaring the model invalid is valid but underdeveloped. Critically examine key assumptions (e.g. constant rates) and propose alternative approaches.
Note the symmetry of the hornet ODE to the bee ODE; comment on whether analogous dynamics should be expected.
In-line grouping of equation 12 and 13 is visually cluttered. Separate each equation on its own line with individual numbering.
The simultaneous equations for the hornet model are in one block, which is confusing. Label and separate each for clarity.
Recognizing that the hornet population curve crosses into negative values and noting the need to restrict t-domain shows thoughtful awareness of model limits.
Limitations are acknowledged but no model refinements are proposed. Recommend exploring logistic growth or carrying-capacity terms.
The conclusion restates earlier sections and is verbose. Condense to highlight main mathematical findings and insights.
Extend personal reflection by proposing data-collection or experimental validation (e.g. field counts, pheromone response) to support parameter choices.
